narrative_blocks.header: He financed Broadway shows and wrote
narrative_blocks.body: Albert Stimson Ingalls Jr. was a playwright and arts patron who produced THE ROOF at the Cleveland Play House and financed several Broadway productions. His older brother David was the Navy's only WWI ace; their father ran the New York Central Railroad. Albert chose theater.

narrative_blocks.header: He financed Broadway shows and wrote
narrative_blocks.body: Albert Stimson Ingalls Jr. was a playwright and arts patron who produced THE ROOF at the Cleveland Play House and financed several Broadway productions. His older brother David was the Navy's only WWI ace; their father ran the New York Central Railroad. Albert chose theater.

parents.father_research_notes: Melville Ezra Ingalls — president of the Big Four Railroad; right-hand man for Cornelius Vanderbilt in the Midwest (New York Central)

notable.notable_blurb: VP and General Manager New York Central Railroad

narrative_blocks.header: From a locomotive cab to railroad VP
narrative_blocks.body: He began in the cab of a locomotive and rose to vice president and general manager of the New York Central’s western lines, retiring after thirty-five years. His father had been Vanderbilt’s man in the Midwest, president of the Big Four.

narrative_blocks.header: The trains were forbidden to whistle near him
narrative_blocks.body: In Bratenahl, Ohio, where he lived, the New York Central’s trains were directed not to sound their whistles passing through. He was the second man in the Cleveland area to own an automobile.

career.role: VP and General Manager, western lines

narrative_blocks.header: She was a sculptor and watercolorist
narrative_blocks.body: Jane Ellison Taft Ingalls was an artist in her own right — a sculptress and painter whose watercolors and sculpture were exhibited at Cleveland May Shows. She was a trustee of the Cleveland Museum of Art, the Cleveland Institute of Art, and the Cleveland Institute of Music. After her mother Anna Sinton Taft's death in the 1930s, she became a part-owner of the Cincinnati Times-Star — the family newspaper long identified with the Tafts.

institutions.institution_blurb: Phillips Academy Andover. Yale 1864; Columbia Law 1866.
Donated $150,000 to The Taft School in 1929; CPT building named for him.
Co-founded with wife Anna Sinton; bequeathed house and 690 artworks to Cincinnati.

cross_connections.display_label: His wife Anna Sinton's father David Sinton left her $20 million — Ohio's largest fortune in 1900. Sinton money financed the presidential bid.

notable.notable_blurb: Owned the Chicago Cubs and Philadelphia Phillies

narrative_blocks.header: He owned the Cubs and the Phillies
narrative_blocks.body: Charles Phelps Taft became a minority owner of the Chicago Cubs in 1905 and helped fund the purchase of the Philadelphia Phillies in 1909 — publicly denying the connection until it became untenable. After serving one term as a congressman and building the Cincinnati Times-Star into the foundation of a media empire, his greatest business legacy was baseball. He sold the Cubs after the 1915 season and his estate, the Taft Museum of Art, is now open to the public.

narrative_blocks.header: His half-brother accepted the presidential nomination
narrative_blocks.body: William Howard Taft accepted the 1908 Republican presidential nomination standing on the portico of Charles Phelps Taft's Cincinnati home at 316 Pike Street — the house their father-in-law David Sinton had purchased and that Charles and Anna later bequeathed to the public as the Taft Museum of Art. The nomination scene on that portico is one of the moments that cement the house's place in American political history.

narrative_blocks.header: She donated $5 million to the University
narrative_blocks.body: Anna Sinton Taft inherited $20 million from her father David Sinton and spent much of her life building one of the most significant private art collections in the American West. In 1927, she and Charles Phelps Taft bequeathed their home and 690 artworks to the people of Cincinnati. The year after he died in 1929, she donated $5 million to the University of Cincinnati, establishing what became the Charles Phelps Taft Research Center.

institutions.institution_blurb: Co-founded with husband Charles Phelps Taft; bequeathed house and 690 artworks to Cincinnati.
